characters.name: Lot and his daughters (small figures fleeing)

symbols: fire and brimstone from a red sky — divine judgment raining down from heaven
burning cities — the total destruction of unrepentant wickedness
rocky cliff separating foreground from destruction — the boundary between salvation and judgment
small fleeing figures — Lot and his family escaping the condemned cities
river — separation between the realm of the living and the realm of destruction
dark smoke — the aftermath of divine wrath consuming all wickedness
description: A dramatic panoramic landscape showing the destruction of cities by fire. The left side of the painting is dominated by a lurid red and orange sky with flames and fiery rain descending upon burning buildings and structures. Dark smoke billows upward. The right side features towering white and gray rocky cliffs with sparse vegetation. Small figures are visible on the right side, apparently fleeing from the destruction. A river or body of water runs through the middle distance, separating the burning cities from the rocky refuge.
theological_description: The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19:24-25) stands as one of the most dramatic acts of divine judgment in Scripture: 'Then the Lord rained on Sodom and Gomorrah sulfur and fire from the Lord out of heaven. And he overthrew those cities, and all the valley, and all the inhabitants of the cities, and what grew on the ground.' The painting captures this cataclysmic moment with terrifying beauty. The narrative is preceded by Abraham's intercession (Genesis 18:22-33), where God agreed to spare the cities if even ten righteous were found — but ten could not be found. This establishes the theology of intercession and the limits of divine patience. The small fleeing figures represent Lot and his daughters, delivered by angelic warning (Genesis 19:15-17). Jesus himself invoked this event as a warning of future judgment: 'Just as it was in the days of Lot... on the day when Lot went out from Sodom, fire and sulfur rained from heaven and destroyed them all — so will it be on the day when the Son of Man is revealed' (Luke 17:28-30). Peter also uses Sodom as a paradigm of judgment: God 'turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to ashes he condemned them to extinction, making them an example of what is going to happen to the ungodly' (2 Peter 2:6). The landscape format emphasizes the cosmic scope of divine judgment.

scripture_refs.reason: The Lord rained sulfur and fire on Sodom and Gomorrah — the event directly depicted
The angels urging Lot to flee before the destruction — the fleeing figures in the painting
Jesus using Sodom's destruction as a warning of the coming judgment at the Son of Man's revelation
Sodom and Gomorrah as an example of what will happen to the ungodly
Abraham's intercession for Sodom — the theological prelude establishing the failure to find the righteous
Sodom and Gomorrah serving as an example by undergoing punishment of eternal fire

_meta.title: Landscape with the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah
